subject: mathematics
prose_summary:
Section 13.1 defines the basic ideas of statistical thinking and reasons about what makes a question statistical. Beginning with guessing gender from height, it introduces the statistical statement — a claim or summary about a phenomenon expressed in numerical values, proportions, probabilities, or predictions — through everyday examples about batting consistency, cycling time, and village population. It then defines a statistical question as one that can be answered by collecting and analysing data (such as 'How tall are Grade 7 students in our school?' or 'Are onions typically costlier in Yahapur or Wahapur?'), noting that such questions expect variation in the answers and so require data to be gathered, analysed, and turned into conclusions. Students are asked to classify a list of everyday questions as statistical or not, before the section names statistics as the study of collecting, organising, analysing, interpreting, and presenting data, and sets up the chapter's aim of using data and graphs to answer such questions.

Section 13.2 applies the ideas of representative value and reasons about which measure best summarises data. Using cricket scores of two players across series of different lengths, it weighs several proposed comparisons (highest single score, total runs, consistency) and shows the total can mislead when the two players played different numbers of matches, and introduces the arithmetic mean (average) as the sum of values divided by their number, understood also as fair-share — worked through guava-sharing groups and daily hibiscus blooms — and notes ancient Indian terms for the mean (samamiti, samarajju by Brahmagupta, samīkaraṇa by Mahāvīrācārya, sāmya by Śrīpati). It then examines the onion-price data of Yahapur and Wahapur, describing data by its minimum, maximum, total, range, and average and visualising it with a dot plot that sorts values and reveals clustering and spread. Through family-height, short-story, and newspaper-page examples it introduces the median (the middle value of sorted data, or the average of the two middle values), the outlier (a value that deviates greatly from the rest), and shows how an outlier pulls the mean away from the median, so that a low outlier gives mean < median and a high outlier gives mean > median. Naming mean and median as measures of central tendency — the tendency of values to pile up around a central value — it also stresses variability, the extremes (minimum and maximum) of the data. It works through the heights of a whole class split into boys and girls, a minute-estimation activity comparing two groups, a 'zero median but high total' cricket example, and zero-versus-no-value cases (a player scoring 0 versus not playing, and a mango tree's off-season months) where only the relevant values should be counted, and closes with a historical note on how a 16-foot 'rod' in 1500s Europe was effectively the arithmetic mean of sixteen men's feet.

Section 13.3 applies graphical representations and reasons about what they show. It combines the two single-column onion-price graphs into one clustered (double) column graph with bars side by side and a stated scale, then presents a two-step process for reading graphs — identify what is given (organisation, scale, patterns) and infer from what is given (analyse and interpret) — applied to worldwide rocket-launch data by organisation and year and to monthly daylight-hour data for Helsinki and Wellington, drawing out that their inverted seasonal patterns reflect opposite hemispheres. It also notes design choices — using colour and pattern fills for greyscale readability, and keeping a time order for months but not for unordered categories such as organisations. A cricket runs-per-over double bar graph with wicket markers is read to answer questions about who batted first, over-by-over scores, and the target. Its Figure it Out set has students read an animal-speed infographic and construct double-bar graphs for superpower preferences across two grades, two days of Jodhpur temperatures, and electric-vehicle registrations across several states and years.

Section 13.4 reasons about the stories and mysteries data can reveal and applies analysis to rich real datasets. Comparing boy and girl heights across grades and schools, and a survey table of average heights of Indian boys and girls aged 5 to 19 across 1989–2019, it asks which statements can be justified from the data, between which successive ages boys and girls grow the most, and how to extrapolate the trend to 2029 — training students to separate claims the data supports from those it does not, and cautioning that one or two schools' data cannot be generalised to a whole country. Its extensive Figure it Out set includes dot-plot comparisons, a scoring table with a did-not-play entry, quiz-score comparison, several double-bar-graph constructions, grouping students by a threshold height, mean-height inference across sections, and a series of individual, long-term, and small-group data-collection projects (sentence and name lengths, an 'In and Out' tracker, family heights, and time estimation), closing with a number-lock deduction puzzle.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 13.1 p.98
enumerated_exercises.description: Decide which of the following are statistical questions: (a) price of a tennis ball in India; (b) ages of dogs on this street; (c) fraction of the class who like walking up a hill; (d) do you like reading; (e) approximately how many bricks are in this wall; (f) best bowler in yesterday's match; (g) last year's rainfall pattern in Barmer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 13.2 p.101
enumerated_exercises.description: Shreyas bounces a ball on a bat with counts 6, 2, 9, 5, 4, 6, 3, 5 over 8 attempts; calculate the average number of bounces.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 13.2 p.101
enumerated_exercises.description: Collect your own bounce data for 7 or more attempts and find the average.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 13.2 p.101
enumerated_exercises.description: Track the number of flowers blooming each day over a week on a neighbourhood plant and find the average per day.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 13.2 p.101
enumerated_exercises.description: Given weekly 100 m times for Nikhil (17, 18, 17, 16, 19, 17, 18) and Sunil (20, 18, 18, 17, 16, 16, 17), decide who on average ran quicker.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 13.2 p.101
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the mean enrolment given six years' figures 1555, 1670, 1750, 2013, 2040, 2126.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 13.2 p.112
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the median of the onion prices in Yahapur and in Wahapur.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 13.2 p.112
enumerated_exercises.description: For Sanskruti's pet-count data (with some absent students), find the mean and median and describe the data.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 13.2 p.112
enumerated_exercises.description: For Rintu's date-palm heights, fill the dot plot, mark the mean and median, describe the heights, find quicker ways to compute the mean, and count trees shorter than the average.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 13.2 p.112
enumerated_exercises.description: Given daily water-usage values, (a) decide whether the mean or median can lie between 25 and 30, justifying using their meaning; (b) decide whether either can be less than the minimum or greater than the maximum value.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 13.2 p.112
enumerated_exercises.description: Fill a dot plot for the given newborn-baby weights of boys and girls, then analyse and compare the data.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 13.2 p.113
enumerated_exercises.description: From the dot plots and central-tendency measures of another Grade 5 section, share observations and compare the heights of the two sections.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 13.2 p.113
enumerated_exercises.description: Given weights of sumo wrestlers and ballet dancers, estimate approximately how many times heavier a sumo wrestler is than a ballet dancer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 13.3 p.122
enumerated_exercises.description: From an animal-speed infographic: (a) state the scale; (b) note what is interesting; (c) find a pair where one speed is about twice the other; (d) judge whether a sailfish is about 4 times faster than a humpback whale and the fastest aquatic animal.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 13.3 p.124
enumerated_exercises.description: From the superpower-preference responses of Grade 5 and Grade 9, draw a double-bar graph comparing the two grades' choices with an appropriate scale.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 13.3 p.124
enumerated_exercises.description: From the two-day temperature data for Jodhpur, draw a double-bar graph with scale 1 unit = 4°C and guess the two months.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 13.3 p.124–125
enumerated_exercises.description: From the clustered-bar graph of electric-vehicle registrations: mark the Gujarat and Delhi bars from the table, describe changes 2022–2024, and answer comparison questions about several states.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 13.4 p.129
enumerated_exercises.description: From the pocket-count dot plots for boys and girls, decide which statements about variability, median, mean, and maximum are true.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 13.4 p.129–130
enumerated_exercises.description: From a table of points over four games with a 'did not play' entry: (a) find A's average per game; (b) decide whether to divide C's and B's totals by 3 or 4 and why; (c) decide who is the best performer.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 13.4 p.130
enumerated_exercises.description: Compare two groups' General Knowledge quiz scores using mean and median and describe their performance.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 13.4 p.130
enumerated_exercises.description: From survey data of watching vs participating in five sports, choose a scale, draw a double-bar graph, and write observations.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 13.4 p.130
enumerated_exercises.description: For 17 students' heights, suggest a threshold height that splits the class into two equal groups, and estimate their age from the 'Telling Tall Tales' table.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 13.4 p.130
enumerated_exercises.description: Describe the mean and median of your class's heights, visualising them on a dot plot.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 13.4 p.130
enumerated_exercises.description: Two 7th-grade sections each have 15 boys and 15 girls; given one section's mean height is 154.2 cm, choose what must be true about the other section's mean.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 13.4 p.131
enumerated_exercises.description: From a skyscraper infographic: (a) estimate the number of skyscrapers in New York, Tokyo, and London; (b) judge the validity of statements about cities relative to Mumbai and the world's tallest building.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 13.4 p.131
enumerated_exercises.description: Estimate and then measure the listed objects, draw a double-bar graph, and find the average difference between estimated and measured values.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q10, section 13.4 p.132
enumerated_exercises.description: For Aditi's Sudoku-solving times over two weeks: (a) construct a dot plot for both weeks; (b) describe the mean, median, and observations.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q11, section 13.4 p.132–133
enumerated_exercises.description: Individual project: either 'How Long is a Sentence?' (dot-plot sentence lengths from two textbook pages and compare with mean/median) or 'What is in a Name?' (mean/median name length, popular starting letters, median starting letter, and a double-bar graph of vowel/consonant patterns).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q12, section 13.4 p.133
enumerated_exercises.description: Individual long-term project 'In and Out': track how many times you step out of your house each day for a month, make a dot plot, and describe the variability, central tendency, and any interesting observations.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q13, section 13.4 p.133
enumerated_exercises.description: Small-group project: either 'Our heights vs. our family's heights' (dot plot and double-bar graph of student and family heights) or 'Estimating time' (dot plots and double-bar graph of 1-minute and 3-minute estimates), then pool and analyse everyone's data.
